participants.name: I (kl4qrd)
participants.professional_persona: I am a senior data scientist at a large healthcare provider, building predictive models for patient readmission risks. My PhD in computational biology drove me to apply AI to real-world clinical problems.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Advanced in R and Python, skilled in survival analysis and deep learning for medical imaging. I also design experiments to validate AI tools in clinical settings.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: I want to bridge the gap between AI research and clinical adoption, eventually shaping national health AI policies. I hope to start a health-tech startup that focuses on preventive care.

participants.name: Mr. (3rh8s7)
participants.professional_persona: Mr. Rajan is a lead AI researcher at a fintech startup, building generative models for fraud detection. He is a regular hackathon winner and uses the AI Trailblazers programme to test new ideas.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Strong in generative adversarial networks, anomaly detection, and rapid prototyping with cloud AI toolkits.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Plans to launch his own AI consulting firm focusing on financial services and to contribute to open‑source AI safety tools.

participants.name: Kumar (4xcf13)
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-4xcf13wy
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-4xcf13wy
participants.professional_persona: Kumar is a hawker who runs a popular chicken rice stall. He wants to use AI to forecast demand and reduce food waste, while keeping his recipes authentic.
participants.skills_and_expertise: He has no formal tech training but is highly resourceful, having attended a 'SMEs Go Digital' workshop. He uses a rudimentary POS system and is open to trial AI tools.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Kumar hopes to implement a simple demand prediction tool that integrates with his ordering system. He wants to prove that AI can help traditional businesses thrive.

participants.name: Dr. (zk6rlv)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Seah is a senior applied scientist at a global tech firm’s Singapore R&D centre, focusing on computer vision for medical imaging. She frequently partners with public hospitals and 100E to validate clinical AI tools.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep knowledge of convolutional and vision transformer models, medical image segmentation, and regulatory validation.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aspires to lead a national‑level AI‑for‑health initiative and bridge the gap between research and clinical deployment.

participants.name: Ling
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-oz05svat
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-oz05svat
participants.professional_persona: Ling is a data analyst in a government statutory board working on urban planning. She is excited about using AI for predictive modelling of traffic and population density.
participants.skills_and_expertise: She holds a degree in statistics and is proficient in R, Python, and GIS tools. She has completed a course on responsible AI through the Civil Service College.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Ling aims to lead a cross-agency AI pilot for smart traffic management. She wants to ensure the models are fair and transparent, in line with the AI for Public Good principle.

initial_posts.narrative: As a lead AI researcher in a fintech startup, I see NAIS 2.0 as a massive boost for AI innovation. The policy commits over S$500 million for AI R&D, which aligns with the resource logic. The 100 Experiments programme caps co-funding at S$330,000 per project, which is generous for prototyping. For compute, I estimate around 1 million GPU hours based on the government's push for infrastructure, though this is not explicitly stated. Trainers are likely around 200, given the need to support enterprises and practitioners through programmes like AIAP and TeSA. These inputs are crucial for enabling projects like mine in fraud detection.
evidence: The policy context states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', so total_funding_sgd is 500,000,000.
The resource logic explicitly says 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so max_co_funding_per_project_sgd is 330,000.
Given the emphasis on compute infrastructure and the scale of AI experimentation, I estimate 1,000,000 GPU hours as a reasonable allocation for the 100E programme and related activities.
Based on the need to train 15,000 practitioners and the existing AIAP and TeSA programmes, I estimate 200 trainers to guide enterprises and practitioners.

initial_posts.narrative: As a senior applied scientist working on medical imaging AI, I see NAIS 2.0's inputs as concrete and well-aligned with the needs of translational research. The S$500 million total funding is explicitly stated in the policy, so I take that as ground truth. The S$330,000 cap per 100E project is also explicit and matches what I've seen in practice—my own clinical AI validation projects typically require around S$250,000 for data annotation, compute, and regulatory work, so this cap is generous but reasonable. For compute, I estimate 1 million GPU hours based on the government's investment in infrastructure and the typical allocation for a national AI programme of this scale; my team uses about 10,000 GPU hours per project, so this would support roughly 100 projects. The trainer count of 200 is my estimate: given the need to support enterprises and practitioners through programmes like AIAP and TeSA, and considering the 100E programme's focus on pairing researchers with companies, 200 trainers seems adequate for a targeted rollout. I note that both Mr. (3rh8s7) and Ling provided similar estimates, which aligns with my independent reasoning from a practitioner's perspective.

initial_posts.narrative: From my perspective as a senior data scientist at a large healthcare provider, the 100E programme's activities must be grounded in the practical realities of engaging enterprises and researchers. With S$500 million in funding and 200 trainers, I estimate 40 enterprise outreach sessions. This is lower than Ling's 50 because, in my experience, meaningful engagement with healthcare enterprises requires deeper, more targeted sessions rather than broad outreach; we need to identify specific problem statements that have no off-the-shelf AI solution, which takes time and careful curation. For researcher matchmaking events, I estimate 15, as pairing researchers with enterprise challenges is a complex process that benefits from smaller, more focused events to ensure quality matches. Ling's 20 seems optimistic given the need for thorough vetting of both enterprise problems and researcher expertise. Training workshops at 30 sessions reflect the need to upskill both trainers and participating organizations; with 200 trainers, each workshop can train about 6-7 organizations, which aligns with the scale of 100E's target of 100 experiments. Platform onboarding assists at 80 are based on the technical complexity of onboarding onto an AI experimentation platform; each assist requires significant trainer time, and with 200 trainers, 80 assists is a reasonable allocation for the first year, considering that not all enterprises will need intensive support.
evidence: My experience in healthcare AI projects shows that enterprise outreach requires deep, targeted sessions to identify viable problem statements, so 40 sessions is a realistic estimate for a national programme aiming for 100 experiments.

Researcher matchmaking events are resource-intensive and benefit from smaller, focused events; 15 events allow for thorough pairing of researchers with enterprise challenges, leveraging the 200 trainers effectively.

Training workshops at 30 sessions align with the need to upskill 200 trainers and participating organizations, assuming each workshop trains about 6-7 organizations to support the 100E programme's scale.

initial_posts.narrative: As a hawker, I see the 100E programme as a chance for small businesses like mine to get AI help. With S$500 million and 200 trainers, I think we need about 40 outreach sessions. That's because many hawkers and small shops need personal, hands-on talks to understand how AI can help with things like reducing food waste. Ling's 50 seems too many—we don't have time for big events, and quality matters more. For matchmaking, 15 events make sense because pairing researchers with our specific problems takes careful matching, not rushed meetings. Training workshops at 30 sessions fit the 200 trainers and the target of 100 experiments—each workshop can train a few businesses at a time. Platform onboarding at 80 assists is enough for the first year, since many of us need step-by-step help to set up AI tools, but not everyone will need it right away.
evidence: As a hawker with no tech background, I know that meaningful outreach to small businesses requires smaller, targeted sessions to explain AI benefits, so 40 sessions is realistic.
With 200 trainers and a target of 100 experiments, 30 training workshops allow each workshop to train about 6-7 organizations, which matches the scale of the programme.
Platform onboarding is complex for non-tech users like me, so 80 assists allocate trainer time efficiently for the first year, given that not all enterprises need intensive support.
Researcher matchmaking at 15 events ensures quality pairings, as my experience with the 'SMEs Go Digital' workshop showed that focused events lead to better outcomes than larger, less targeted ones.

initial_posts.narrative: As a hawker, I see the 100E programme as a chance for small businesses like mine to get AI help. With 70 approved projects from the outputs phase, I think about 50 will actually be deployed into production because technical hurdles and data access issues slow things down—my own demand forecasting tool took months to test. The AI practitioner pool is growing but not yet at 15,000; with 120 trainees completing the programme and other upskilling efforts, I estimate around 12,000 practitioners, as many small businesses like mine are still learning. For generative AI use cases, the AI Trailblazers programme aims for 100 in 100 days, but I think only about 80 will be created because some enterprises struggle to define clear use cases. Enterprise AI adoption rate is still low—maybe 12.5%—because most hawkers and SMEs are cautious and need more hand-holding, as I saw at the 'SMEs Go Digital' workshop.
